name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, operating as a vertically integrated steelmaker from iron ore mining through pellet and HBI production to primary steelmaking and downstream finishing, stamping, tooling, and tubing. The company serves automotive, infrastructure, manufacturing, and distributor end markets. Founded in 1847, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio. Formerly Cliffs Natural Resources Inc., the company transformed through acquisitions of AK Steel (2020) and ArcelorMittal USA (2021).

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed two transformative acquisitions — AK Steel in 2020 and ArcelorMittal USA in 2021 — pivoting from a pure iron ore miner to North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer, a fundamental business model transformation [1][2]. However, no significant new product launches or market-creating innovations since the integration was completed. AI Deployment (1/2): CLF announced a three-year partnership with Palantir in April 2026 to deploy AI across production planning, order entry, and operational workflows; CEO Goncalves called early results 'a gamechanger,' but no quantified cost savings or revenue impact has been reported yet [3]. Market Share (2/2): Largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America with dominant automotive-grade steel market position, serving approximately 50% of the domestic automotive steel market [1][2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): FY2026 guidance includes steel unit cost reductions of ~$10/net ton and shipment volumes of 16.5-17.0 million net tons, signaling operational improvement, but no next-generation product launches disclosed [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is in line with steel industry norms; the COO oversees a Research and Innovation Center, but spend is not above-average for the sector.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs' vision is 'to set the standard for safe, sustainable, and secure North American steel — leading in automotive-grade quality, supply assurance, and lower-carbon metallurgy' [4]. This is clear and specific to the company's role, connecting steel production to sustainability and supply security, but it is ultimately an industry-positioning statement rather than a transformative purpose that inspires sacrifice. Community Impact (1/2): The company publishes comprehensive sustainability reports aligned with GRI and SASB standards, committed to 25% GHG emissions reduction by 2030 from 2017 levels, and operates community engagement programs across its facility footprint, but no distinctive foundation or signature charitable initiative was identified [5]. Employee Retention (0/2): With approximately 25,000 employees and a heavily unionized workforce (United Steelworkers), no specific voluntary turnover data or employee tenure metrics were found. Insider Buying (0/2): CEO Lourenco Goncalves sold 3 million shares ($37.3M) in February 2026 through an estate planning trust, representing nearly half his holdings; CFO Celso Goncalves Jr. also sold $2.87M in shares — significant net insider selling with no offsetting insider purchases identified [6].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B (up 6.3% YoY from $4.6B) showed improvement from a weak 2025, but full-year 2025 saw $18.6B revenue (down from $19.2B in 2024) and a GAAP net loss of $1.4B ($2.91/share) [1]. The balance sheet carries D/E of 129.3%, well above the 70% target but below the 300% floor, scoring 0.74. Negative ROE of -18.6% and negative profit margin of -6.4% both score 0.0, reflecting the cyclical trough in steel pricing. Forward P/E of 27.4 scores 0.81, and the forward PEG of 0.43 is exceptionally attractive, scoring at the maximum 1.0 and reflecting expected earnings recovery as steel prices normalize [1]. Average daily volume of 18.1M shares at $13.78 provides ample liquidity ($249M daily turnover). Revenue Growth 5yr CAGR excluded due to data discontinuity from transformative acquisitions.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Steel production is a physical-world, capital-intensive manufacturing process that AI structurally cannot substitute — blast furnaces, electric arc furnaces, hot-rolling mills, and mining operations require massive physical infrastructure, skilled labor, and regulatory compliance that are immune to AI displacement [2]. Moreover, AI infrastructure buildout (data centers, power generation, electrical grid expansion) is a direct demand driver for steel products, making CLF a potential beneficiary of AI-driven capital expenditure. Analysts have not flagged AI as a disruption risk for integrated steelmakers. The company's automotive steel franchise is further protected by OEM qualification processes and decades-long supplier relationships that cannot be replicated digitally. AI Opportunity (0/1): While CLF announced a Palantir partnership in April 2026 to deploy AI in production planning and operations, this is an internal efficiency tool, not an AI revenue-generating product [3]. No AI-related revenue has been reported.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under CEO Lourenco Goncalves is a quintessentially finite player. Goncalves has executed brilliantly on acquisitions and vertical integration, but his leadership style is combative, short-term oriented, and centered on 'winning' against competitors rather than advancing a durable cause. His confrontational earnings calls, aggressive language toward rivals and short-sellers, and the massive $37M insider stock sale in February 2026 signal a leader playing to win this cycle rather than building an institution designed to endure across generations.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Cleveland-Cliffs has a stated vision of setting 'the standard for safe, sustainable, and secure North American steel,' which connects the company's work to supply security and decarbonization themes [4]. However, this vision was articulated primarily after the 2020-2021 acquisitions and has not been tested through crisis or multi-decade consistency. The purpose reads more as strategic positioning than as a cause that inspires sacrifice — Goncalves' public commentary focuses overwhelmingly on market dynamics, competitor weaknesses, and shareholder returns rather than advancing this vision.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): CEO Goncalves is widely known for confrontational, aggressive behavior on earnings calls — berating analysts, attacking short-sellers, and using combative language that Sinek would identify as antithetical to psychological safety [2]. The company has experienced significant executive turnover, and the top-down, CEO-centric culture raises questions about whether employees feel safe to take risks and admit mistakes. The heavily unionized workforce (~25,000 employees) operates in an adversarial management-labor structure that limits the kind of trust Sinek envisions.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves' competitive language is quintessentially finite — he frequently attacks competitors (Nucor, Steel Dynamics) and short-sellers by name, framing competition as a war to be won rather than a force for self-improvement [2]. His public commentary focuses on dominating the market and destroying rivals rather than learning from their strengths. Sinek would identify this as a textbook finite competitive mindset.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs executed one of the most dramatic strategic pivots in American industrial history — transforming from a pure iron ore mining company (Cliffs Natural Resources) into North America's largest integrated flat-rolled steelmaker through the acquisitions of AK Steel (March 2020, during COVID) and ArcelorMittal USA (December 2020) [1][2]. This was a fundamental reinvention of the company's identity, business model, and market position. The pivot was driven by Goncalves' conviction that vertical integration was essential for long-term competitiveness, representing genuine existential flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves demonstrated significant courage in acquiring AK Steel during the onset of COVID-19 (March 2020) when steel prices were collapsing and markets were panicking — a $1.1B bet on vertical integration that most analysts questioned at the time [1][2]. Following up with the $1.4B ArcelorMittal USA acquisition months later doubled down on the strategy. However, the courage appears more financial and opportunistic than cause-driven, and the massive $37M insider stock sale in February 2026 undercuts the narrative of long-term conviction [6].

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs offers deep cyclical value as North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer trading at $13.78 with a forward PEG of 0.43, reflecting significant earnings recovery potential as steel prices normalize from cyclical lows [1]. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B grew 6.3% YoY with Adjusted EBITDA of $95M (vs. -$179M in Q1 2025), and management guides for sequential quarterly improvement through 2026 with 16.5-17.0 million net tons of shipments and $10/ton cost reductions [1]. The Palantir AI partnership could drive meaningful operational efficiency gains across the company's 12+ facilities [3]. Tariff protections (Section 232) and Buy America provisions create a structural demand floor for domestic steel, and automotive EV adoption increases steel content per vehicle.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on existential financial risk: CLF posted a $1.4B net loss in FY2025, carries D/E of 129.3%, and operates with negative profit margins of -6.4% and negative ROE of -18.6% [1]. CEO Goncalves' $37.3M insider stock sale in February 2026 — representing nearly half his holdings — is a concerning signal from the company's most vocal advocate [6]. Revenue has declined from $23.0B in 2022 to $18.6B in 2025, reflecting structural weakness in steel pricing and demand. Forward P/E of 27.4 is elevated for a loss-making steel producer, suggesting the market does not expect rapid earnings normalization. The heavy fixed-cost structure amplifies losses during downturns, and a prolonged steel pricing weakness or recession could make the debt load unsustainable, potentially forcing dilutive capital raises.
key_catalysts: Steel price recovery from cyclical lows driving margin expansion — every $50/ton increase in HRC prices adds approximately $800M in annual EBITDA given 16.5M+ ton shipment volume
Palantir AI partnership delivering measurable cost reductions across steelmaking operations, with CEO calling pilot results 'a gamechanger' and full deployment underway across all facilities
Section 232 tariff extensions and expanded Buy America infrastructure spending creating a sustainable price floor for domestically produced flat-rolled steel
key_risks: D/E of 129.3% with negative free cash flow and negative profit margins creates existential balance sheet risk if the steel cycle does not recover within 12-18 months
Revenue decline from $23.0B (2022) to $18.6B (2025) and $1.4B GAAP net loss suggest structural overcapacity pressure in the North American flat-rolled steel market

CEO Goncalves' $37.3M insider stock sale and combative leadership style create governance risk — the company is heavily dependent on a single executive whose confrontational approach alienates analysts and institutional investors
